ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tart class; no chocolate-tart-specific term exists. Closest parent class used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pastries and cakes as a category; no chocolate-tart-specific code exists. Closest parent category code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for chocolate tart identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser.

main_ingredients: pastry shell (wheat flour, butter, sugar, egg)
chocolate ganache or chocolate custard (dark chocolate, cream, sugar, eggs)

serving.description: 1 slice (approximately 1/8 of a standard 20 cm tart)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of chocolate tart is one slice weighing approximately 80 g, equivalent to one-eighth of a standard 20 cm round tart.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate tart — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as sourced from HPB Foodo. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chocolate tart (USDA SR Legacy item 18354 / closest analogue) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; see notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chocolate and Chocolate-Based Confections — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate; chocolate-based pastry GI range 35–45
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for chocolate tart exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is estimated from the closest analogous entries: chocolate cake (GI ~38, University of Sydney) and shortcrust pastry-based tarts (GI ~40–45). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 27.12 g) / 100 = 10.8, rounded to 10.9. Available carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 33.9 × 0.80 = 27.12 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (20.4 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (~14.8 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chocolate tart to occasional small portions (≤40 g) and avoid pairing with other high-sugar or high-sodium foods, as the high sugar load independently elevates blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 20.4 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; refined pastry carbohydrates and high saturated fat further impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit chocolate tart due to its high sugar content (20.4 g/100 g) and refined carbohydrate load; if consumed, restrict to a single small slice (≤40 g) paired with a protein or high-fibre food to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~14.8 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) from butter-based pastry raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherogenesis
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat chocolate tart as an occasional indulgence only; the high saturated fat (~14.8 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) from the butter-based pastry shell significantly raise LDL cholesterol and should be counted against the daily saturated fat budget (≤7% of total energy per AHA guidelines).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate tart is a baked Western dessert pastry consisting of a short-crust or pâte sucrée (sweet pastry) shell filled with rich chocolate ganache or chocolate custard. The pastry shell is made from wheat flour, butter, sugar, and eggs, producing a crisp, buttery base. The filling is typically prepared by combining melted dark or semi-sweet chocolate with heavy cream and sometimes eggs to form a dense, silky ganache, or with eggs and milk to form a baked chocolate custard. The result is a dessert with a firm, crumbly pastry exterior and a smooth, intensely chocolatey interior. Per 100 g, chocolate tart provides approximately 432 kcal, 29.5 g fat (of which ~14.8 g is saturated), 33.9 g carbohydrate, 20.4 g sugar, 7.6 g protein, and 3.3 g dietary fibre. The high saturated fat and sugar content make it a high-concern food for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), but the glycemic load per standard 80 g serving is approximately 10.9, which is moderate. Chocolate tart is widely available in cafes, patisseries, and supermarkets across Singapore and globally. It is typically consumed as a dessert or snack and is not suitable for daily consumption in clinical dietary contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chocolate tart?
Is chocolate tart high in sugar?
What is the GI of chocolate tart?
How much fat is in a chocolate tart?
Is chocolate tart suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore food composition database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and potassium are estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue (chocolate tart / chocolate pastry) and are consistent with the HPB total fat value. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chocolate cake entry.
notes: Saturated fat (14.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (9.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.3 g/100 g), cholesterol_mg (85.0 mg/100 g), and potassium_mg (210.0 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chocolate tart / chocolate pastry (closest approved-source analogue), as HPB Foodo does not separately report these fatty acid fractions for this item. The sum of saturated + monounsaturated + polyunsaturated + trans fat (14.8 + 9.2 + 1.4 + 0.3 = 25.7 g) is consistent with the HPB-reported total fat of 29.52 g/100 g within expected rounding and minor unclassified fat fractions. Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for chocolate tart and are not available in the USDA SR Legacy closest analogue at sufficient specificity for this preparation. The dietary_tags 'high_fibre' in the input is noted as borderline — 3.3 g/100 g meets the Singapore HPB threshold for high fibre (≥3 g/100 g for solid foods) and is retained. The 'low_sodium' tag is valid at 59 mg/100 g. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this specific product has not been identified in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.
